request.question: Who else did the author of The Power Broker write a biography of?

title: The Power Broker
Capitol Punishment (book)
Robert Caro
Captan Jack Wyly
Stephen M. Sweeney
Power broker (politics)
Raymond Lesniak
Joachim Kempin
Edward Dean Adams
Shaun Going
sentences: The Power Broker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York is a Pulitzer Prize-winning biography of Robert Moses by Robert Caro.

The book focuses on the creation and use of power in local and state politics, as witnessed through Moses' use of unelected positions to design and implement dozens of highways and bridges, sometimes at great cost to the communities he nominally served.
It has been repeatedly named one of the best biographies of the 20th century, and has been highly influential on city planners and politicians throughout the United States.

Capitol Punishment: The Hard Truth About Washington Corruption From America's Most Notorious Lobbyist is a non-fiction 300-page memoir by former American lobbyist Jack Abramoff, published by WNDbooks in November 2011.

The book, described as an "account of his political triumphs, serial lawbreaking and unethical conduct" by the "Washington Post", details the author's life in Washington as a power broker and lobbyist.
In its last chapter, titled "Path to Reform", Abramoff lists a number of proposals to eliminate bribery of government officials.
Robert Allan Caro (born October 30, 1935) is an American journalist and author known for his biographies of United States political figures Robert Moses and Lyndon B. Johnson.

Captan Jack Wyly, Sr. (December 30, 1917 – July 14, 2006), was a colorful attorney in Lake Providence, Louisiana, who in the 1960s and 1970s was a leader of conservatives within his state's dominant Democratic Party.
He was known for his 1960s-style suits and hats.
His massive wealth enabled him to act as a power broker over nearly every aspect of Lake Providence affairs.
Stephen M. "Steve" Sweeney (born June 11, 1959) is an American executive, political power broker, and Democratic Party politician who currently serves as the President of the New Jersey Senate.
He has served in the New Jersey State Senate since 2002, where he represents the 3rd Legislative District.
On November 23, 2009, Sweeney was selected as Senate President-designate, replacing former Gov. Richard Codey.
A power broker is a person who influences people to vote towards a particular client (i.e. elected official or referendum) in exchange for political and financial benefits.
Power brokers can also negotiate deals with other power brokers to meet their aims.

Raymond J. "Ray" Lesniak (born May 7, 1946) is an American Democratic Party politician, who has been serving in the New Jersey State Senate since 1983, where he represents the 20th Legislative District.
Before entering New Jersey's upper house, the Senate, Lesniak served in the General Assembly from 1978 to 1983.
Lesniak is considered a major power broker in the Democratic Party, having served as New Jersey Democratic State Chairman and New Jersey Chair for Clinton/Gore and Gore/Lieberman.
Lesniak was a candidate for Governor of New Jersey in the 2017 election.
Joachim Kempin (born in 1942) is a German-born businessman and retired Senior Vice President of Microsoft Corporation.
He ran Microsoft's division selling operating software to PC manufacturers for 15 years.
He is also the author of "Resolve and Fortitude: Microsoft's "Secret Power Broker" Breaks His Silence".
Edward Dean Adams (9 April 1846 – 20 May 1931) was an American businessman, banker, power broker and numismatist.
He was the president of Niagara Falls Hydraulic Power and Manufacturing Company which built the first hydroelectric power plants in Niagara Falls, Canada.
The Adams Power Plant Transformer House is named after him.
He was “conspicuously successful in corporate reorganizations”.
Shaun Going is a Canadian construction engineer who was arrested by Serbia in August 2000 and charged with terrorism.

An acknowledged power broker with "enormous influence known to bribe and fraternise with other powerful figures in the region, including the Kosovo Liberation Army, he was quickly defended by Canada who insisted on his innocence.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who else did the author of The Power Broker write a biography of?
hop1_docs: The Power Broker | The Power Broker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York is a Pulitzer Prize-winning biography of Robert Moses by Robert Caro.  The book focuses on the creation and use of power in local and state politics, as witnessed through Moses' use of unelected positions to design and implement dozens of highways and bridges, sometimes at great cost to the communities he nominally served.  It has been repeatedly named one of the best biographies of the 20th century, and has been highly influential on city planners and politicians throughout the United States.
List of biographers | Biographers are authors who write an account of another person's life, while autobiographers are authors who write their own biography.

Get Happy: The Life of Judy Garland | Get Happy: The Life of Judy Garland is a biography of entertainer Judy Garland.  Published in 2000, "Get Happy" is author Gerald Clarke's follow-up to his 1988 biography of Truman Capote.  Clarke conducted some 500 interviews, including some with subjects who had not previously spoken about Garland, and also drew upon tape recordings that Garland had made in the 1960s for an autobiography.  He found Garland's unpublished 68-page manuscript in the Random House archives.  Clarke spent ten years on the book, and only made his final decision to write about Garland after reading the extant biographies.  "I did not want to write a book about her if the definitive book had already been written...So, I sat down and I read the biographies that had already been written and came up with no real impression of Judy...There was a disconnect between the woman who emerged from the pages and the woman I saw in the movies and heard on the records...I knew that "the" book had not yet been written."

The Broker | The Broker is a suspense novel written by American author John Grisham and published in the United States on January 11, 2005.  The novel follows the story of Joel Backman, a newly pardoned prisoner who had tried to broker a deal to sell the world's most powerful satellite surveillance system to the highest bidder.

Power broker (politics) | A power broker is a person who influences people to vote towards a particular client (i.e. elected official or referendum) in exchange for political and financial benefits.  Power brokers can also negotiate deals with other power brokers to meet their aims.

Janaki Ram | Janaki Ram is an Indian-American author who has written biographies and collections of short stories, as well as a prominent figure in Asian art auctions.  She is a relative of Krishna Menon and Sir C.P. Ramaswami Iyer and a member of the powerful South Indian Vengalil family.  She was a founder and convenor of INTACH previous to her writing career.

Ann Waldron | Ann Wood Waldron (December 14, 1924 – July 2, 2010) was an American author who initially focused on writing for children and young adults, then turned to biographies of authors from the South, and ultimately shifted in her late seventies to writing murder mysteries set at Princeton University.
hop1_titles: Get Happy: The Life of Judy Garland
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: The Power Broker (book) and its author Robert Caro

- Passage [1]: "The Power Broker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York" — a "Pulitzer Prize-winning biography" "of Robert Moses" "by Robert Caro."
- No passage names any other book or biography authored by Robert Caro (missing fact: "Which other biography(ies) Robert Caro wrote?"; bridge entity: Robert Caro).
- Priority next-hop retrievals:
  1. "Robert Caro" (Wikipedia article) — inspect lede/infobox and "Works"/"Bibliography"/"Books" sections.
  2. Search "Robert Caro bibliography" or "Robert Caro works" — fetch list pages or bibliographic sections.
  3. If needed, fetch specific book pages found in those sections (e.g., any titles listed under Caro's works) to confirm they are biographies.
hop2_docs: Robert Caro | Robert Allan Caro (born October 30, 1935) is an American journalist and author known for his biographies of United States political figures Robert Moses and Lyndon B. Johnson.
Robert Holdstock bibliography | This is a bibliography of fantasy author Robert Holdstock.

Caro William | Caro William is a play by William Douglas-Home which premiered at the Embassy Theatre (London) in 1952.  The cast included Robert Shaw as Mr. George Lamb, Rachel Gurney as Mrs. George Lamb and Freda Gaye as Lady Melbourne.  This play was the London stage acting debut of Robert Shaw.

The Last Celt | The Last Celt: A Bio–Bibliography of Robert Ervin Howard is a biography and bibliography of Robert E. Howard by Glenn Lord.  It was first published by Donald M. Grant, Publisher, Inc. in 1976 in an edition of 2,600 copies.
Robert Bresson bibliography | A list of books and essays about Robert Bresson:
Robert Pinsky bibliography | A list of the published work by or about American poet Robert Pinsky.
Robert Silverberg bibliography | List of the published work of Robert Silverberg, American science fiction author.
